Military Mar 28, 2026

6 Reasons Why The F-35 Is So Expensive To Operate

The F-35 is expensive to operate because it is the most capable fighter jet that requires a specialized ecosystem greater than other fighter jets.

The F-35's high operating costs reflect its advanced integrated systems architecture requiring specialized infrastructure and supply chains unavailable for legacy fighter platforms.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 28, 2026

“Today We Mourn”: Pilots Of Air Canada Flight 8646 Honored As Bodies Return Home

The return of Captain Antoine Forest and Co-pilot Mackenzie Gunther's remains to Montreal and Ottawa were met with tributes at the airport.

The tragedy highlights aviation's human cost and the industry's need for enhanced safety protocols to prevent similar accidents involving experienced flight crews.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Why Airlines Retire Aircraft Early

Airlines are retiring young aircraft like the A320neo and A220, primarily due to engine reliability issues and skyrocketing engine values.

Rising engine values create economic incentives for early retirement, as airlines can sell powerplants separately for greater profit than operating aging aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF rollout levy amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical instability disrupting energy markets is prompting governments to recalibrate aviation fuel taxation, signaling economic headwinds for carrier cost management.

See AeroTime Hub →

PAI
Safety Mar 28, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective safety risk management requires organizations to systematically document why residual risks are acceptable rather than simply eliminating all theoretical hazards, balancing operational feasibility with safety objectives.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

NASA's compressed pre-launch timeline for Artemis 2 reflects confidence in vehicle readiness, but tight schedules increase risk exposure for critical human spaceflight missions.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 28, 2026

What Happens When A Pilot Rejects Landing At The Last Second

A last-second rejected landing demands precise aircraft handling and coordination between both pilots and air traffic control.

Go-around procedures consume significant fuel reserves and require immediate ATC coordination to prevent separation conflicts with other traffic in the terminal area.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

The 1 Metric That Determines Airline Profitability

This single factor often determines airline success.

Load factor alone is insufficient for profitability; airlines must balance capacity utilization with yield management to avoid racing to the bottom on margins.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

How to Assess Safety Risk

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5 risk assessment provides a structured methodology for operators to systematically identify and quantify safety hazards before incidents occur.

Military Mar 27, 2026

Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Loss of KC-135 tankers directly threatens U.S. air operations across the Middle East due to critical refueling capacity gaps and extended aircraft maintenance timelines.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Southwest Airlines Adds 42 New Nonstop Routes This Spring [Map]

They include four new cities, with one in particular being historic...

Southwest's expansion into four new cities signals competitive pressure on underserved markets and potential network consolidation shifts among low-cost carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

“AI Can Help”: American Airlines’ Newest Solution To Get Rid Of Contrails

How the airline is working to ensure that contrails become a thing of the past, and why this is important.

American Airlines is leveraging AI to predict and avoid atmospheric conditions that form contrails, addressing a growing sustainability concern that represents an emerging competitive differentiator for climate-conscious carriers.

See Simple Flying →

▶15:03
Safety Mar 28, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Assurance

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety assurance, which evaluates the continued effectiveness of implemented risk control strategies and supports the identification of new hazards. Whether you're an owner, operator or part of a small aviation team, this guide provides valuable insights and tools to enhance your safety management practices and ensure a safer operational environment.

Safety assurance components validate whether risk controls actually work in practice and detect emerging hazards, creating a continuous feedback loop essential for small operators' SMS maturity.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Ground communication infrastructure, managed by specialists like Richards, is as critical to lunar missions as the spacecraft itself, representing decades of career-focused expertise.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 27, 2026

How GE Aerospace Engines Stack Up Against The Rolls-Royce Trent Series

Which will come out on top?

GE and Rolls-Royce's competing turbofan architectures reflect diverging strategies on fuel efficiency, maintenance costs, and environmental compliance that will reshape aircraft economics through 2040.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M commitment to Intuitive Machines demonstrates the agency's shift toward leveraging commercial partners to reduce costs and accelerate lunar logistics for sustained Artemis operations.

See NASA →

▶9:39
Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Risk Management

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety risk management, which determines the need for, and adequacy of, new or revised risk controls based on the assessment of acceptable risk.

Small operators must document risk assessment methodologies to demonstrate how they determine when existing controls are insufficient for acceptable safety levels.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Military Mar 27, 2026

7 Reasons Why The US Air Force Still Operates The B-52

From nuclear deterrence to missile truck missions, the B-52 Stratofortress remains essential to US airpower even in 2026.

The B-52's continued relevance through 2026 demonstrates how platform longevity can be sustained through mission evolution rather than replacement, offering lessons for managing aging military aircraft fleets.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

Deaf Woman Says Disability Caused Flight Removal, But Frontier Airlines Says She Had Alcohol

Viral Frontier removal dispute pits disability-accommodation claims against the airline’s alcohol accusation.

Airlines face increasing litigation risk as disability accommodation disputes intertwine with subjective crew judgment calls on passenger behavior, requiring clearer documentation protocols.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework incentivizes aerospace contractors and partners to embed sustainability practices into operations, potentially raising industry-wide standards for resource stewardship.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 28, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation system levels offers a more immediate pathway to sustainability than technology alone, requiring coordinated industry action.

See MDPI →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen engine parts from scrapped aircraft pose undetectable safety risks, requiring enhanced verification protocols across the entire supply chain.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

CSF expansion into lunar mobility and power generation signals the commercial space sector's maturation toward sustained off-world operations infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

NASA's Gateway halt forces ESA to reassess €1B+ investment timeline, potentially reshaping European participation in lunar exploration infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke Days showcases the operational capabilities and mission diversity of F-16 units at one of the Air Force's largest fighter bases to civilian audiences.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's synthetic aperture radar capability to penetrate cloud cover enables persistent Earth observation critical for volcanic monitoring and disaster preparedness in regions with frequent weather obstruction.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar enables all-weather Earth observation that penetrates cloud cover, expanding aviation weather monitoring and volcanic hazard detection capabilities.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

NTSB Issues Preliminary Report on Hudson River Ditching

Investigators say an instructional flight ended after a loss of oil pressure and total engine failure.

Loss of oil pressure preceded complete engine failure, suggesting potential maintenance or mechanical defects may have contributed to the emergency landing.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Tech and Science Bound for Low Earth Orbit on Commercial Launch

Technology and science demonstrations, supported by various NASA industry collaborations and agency developments, are set to launch to low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the company’s Transporter-16 commercial rideshare mission. These demonstrations will test thermal...

NASA's reliance on commercial rideshare missions reduces launch costs while accelerating technology validation cycles for space-based experiments and demonstrations.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

House Transportation Chair Sam Graves To Retire

Graves helped shape FAA reauthorization, BasicMed and several other general aviation measures.

Loss of a key congressional advocate for general aviation regulatory relief could slow future reforms on BasicMed expansion and FAA modernization efforts.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Names Scientists to Support Lunar South Pole Science

NASA has selected 10 participating scientists to help shape a science plan for astronauts to complete on the lunar surface under the Artemis program – including deploying scientific instruments, making critical observations of the landing site, and collecting Moon rocks. “Congratulations to the...

NASA's selection of participating scientists ensures Artemis lunar missions will generate high-quality scientific data through coordinated instrument deployment and sample collection strategies.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Three killed in Hawaii tour helicopter crash on Kauai

Three people died and two others were injured after an Airborne Aviation tour helicopter crashed on the afternoon…...

Tour helicopter incidents continue highlighting safety risks in commercial sightseeing operations, particularly in challenging island terrain environments.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Michael Guzman

Listen to this audio excerpt from Michael Guzman, Artemis II main propulsion systems engineer: A clue to what Mike Guzman, main propulsion systems engineer at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, loves most can be found in the signature of his work email: a complex string of equations for rocket...

NASA's Artemis II propulsion engineer represents the specialized technical expertise required for deep-space exploration systems that directly influence commercial aerospace supply chains.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

SES meoSphere will be core to new MEO/LEO inflight connectivity

WASHINGTON D.C. — Rapidly putting the wheels in motion to launch a next-generation Medium Earth Orbit satellite network, dubbed meoSphere, SES intends to pair the new service with Low Earth Orbit satcom to power multi-orbit MEO/LEO inflight connectivity for airlines, company CEO Adel Al-Saleh...

SES's hybrid MEO/LEO satellite strategy addresses aviation's latency and capacity demands by combining medium and low earth orbit coverage for superior inflight connectivity performance.

See Runway Girl Network →

Drones Mar 27, 2026

This firmware update makes Avata 360 ready for real flying

The new DJI Avata 360 drone is arriving with a feature-rich day-one firmware update that significantly expands what this immersive FPV drone can do right out of the box. The initial firmware rollout, headlined by v01.00.0100 for the aircraft, arrives alongside updates for the entire ecosystem,...

DJI's day-one firmware update for Avata 360 enables full operational capabilities immediately upon delivery, reducing time-to-flight for commercial FPV operations.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

India’s Bellatrix raises $20 million following overseas expansion drive

India-based Bellatrix Aerospace announced March 27 it has raised $20 million to ramp up production of its satellite propulsion systems after securing its first large commercial customer outside the country.

Bellatrix's overseas customer win validates Indian propulsion technology commercially, signaling emerging competition in the global satellite market beyond traditional aerospace leaders.

See SpaceNews →
